change_summary: detail_verdict_length grew from 845 → 1833 (+117%); driven by a newly-added post-hoc-test paragraph (commit window since c2 on 2026-04-07). claim_length, finding_length, detail_evidence_length unchanged. Sourced from drift-audit.json generated 2026-05-09T01:10:03Z; obe_skipped=4 (WIN-063/066/043/006 already re-reviewed since audit).
our_argument_summary: Our review argues the brightness difference between Sigma Octantis (V=5.42) and Polaris (V=1.98) is entirely explained by intrinsic luminosity — Polaris cited as F7Ib supergiant ~1,260 L☉ at 433 ly vs. Sigma Octantis F0III/IV ~40 L☉ at 294 ly. Verdict text cites Gaia DR3 photometry of 1.8 billion stars showing no systematic residuals exceeding 1%, names three bright southern stars (Canopus, Achernar, Alpha Centauri) consistent with their spectral classifications, and adds (new in this cycle) a second paragraph applying our post-hoc test: no monitor.py entry exists for this WIN, the value is taken from a publicly indexed catalogue available before any prediction was registered, and the legitimate-novelty carve-out of Part 3.1b does not apply.

relevant_files: No code found in any dome repo file for WIN-033
raw-text/05-model.txt OPEN-004 (Polaris southern visibility cutoff — explicitly OPEN)
dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: WIN-033 has no computational pipeline in the dome's codebase. monitor.py contains no domain or function for stellar photometry. The dome's own model.txt lists "OPEN-004: Polaris invisible from southern hemisphere — cutoff formula not derived" as an unsolved open problem, indicating the geometric basis for any pole-related stellar effect has never been worked out.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: Purely qualitative assertion — no magnitude prediction, no extinction formula, no photometric comparison logic.
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: The aetheric refraction index n(r) → 28.8 at the rim could in principle be invoked, but the dome provides no specific extinction model linking n(r) to stellar magnitudes, and OPEN-004 is explicitly flagged as unsolved.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: None — our review correctly states there is no monitor.py automation for this WIN.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: No domain, function, or variable for sigma/octantis/polaris-magnitude/dimness in monitor.py. The dome's 02-wins.txt has only a one-line CONFIRMED claim with no surrounding logic. The dome's own ai-context lists OPEN-004 (Polaris southern cutoff) as unsolved.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: Standard stellar physics (Hertzsprung-Russell classification since the early 1900s) explains the magnitude difference via intrinsic luminosity (supergiant vs. subgiant). The dome relabels this as "aetheric depth at disc edge." Same observed values, renamed cause.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: Sigma Octantis (V=5.42) and Polaris (V=1.98) magnitudes have been catalogued for centuries; Hipparcos/Gaia parallaxes since the 1990s/2010s. The dome adopts these public values and labels the asymmetry a "confirmation" with no prior prediction of a numerical value or threshold.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: No derivation from disc_radius, firmament_height, or n(r) to a predicted magnitude difference. The dome's own OPEN-004 acknowledges that even the simpler geometric question (Polaris visibility cutoff) has no derivation. Any extinction story would therefore inherit an unsolved foundation.

holes_found.description: Persisting Gaia EDR3-vs-DR3 citation mismatch (carried over from c2, not fixed). The detail_verdict_text and the post-hoc paragraph both name "Gaia DR3" but the linked URL (aanda.org/articles/aa/full_html/2021/05/aa39587-20/aa39587-20.html) is Riello et al. 2021 (A&A 649 A3), which is the EDR3 photometric calibration paper, not DR3. By contrast, WIN-017 (parallax = firmament wobble) cites the DR3 release paper correctly via doi.org/10.1051/0004-6361/202243940 (Gaia Collaboration et al. 2023, A&A 674 A1). Same dataset, two different WINs, only one citation correct. A dome defender pasting our review into an LLM would have an LLM trivially flag the mismatch and cast doubt on every other citation. This was c2's only moderate-rated hole; one cycle later it is still open.
holes_found.recommendation: Either (a) replace the Riello 2021 link with the DR3 release paper (doi.org/10.1051/0004-6361/202243940) or with the DR3 photometric validation paper (Montegriffo et al. 2023, A&A 674 A33, doi.org/10.1051/0004-6361/202243880), keeping the "Gaia DR3" wording; or (b) change "Gaia DR3" to "Gaia EDR3" everywhere in detail_verdict_text and keep the Riello 2021 link. Option (a) is preferred because the 1.8-billion-source catalog is most naturally cited as DR3.

holes_found.description: Quantitative inconsistency in the cited intrinsic-luminosity numbers. Plugging the values our text gives — Polaris L ≈ 1,260 L☉ at 433 ly, Sigma Octantis L ≈ 40 L☉ at 294 ly — into F ∝ L/d² yields a predicted apparent magnitude difference Δm ≈ 2.91 magnitudes, but the observed Δm = 5.42 − 1.98 = 3.44 magnitudes. That is a 0.53-magnitude gap (a factor of ≈1.6× in flux) the cited values do not account for. Working backwards from the apparent magnitudes and the cited distances, M_V(Polaris) = 1.98 − 5·log10(132.6 pc/10 pc) = −3.64 → L_V ≈ 2,430 L☉, not 1,260. The 1,260 figure (taken from popular sources) implicitly assumes a shorter Polaris distance (~325 ly) than the 433 ly we cite. Either the L value or the distance is wrong; using both together is internally inconsistent. The phrase "the magnitude difference is entirely intrinsic luminosity" overstates precision by exactly the amount of this gap, and a careful dome defender or LLM cross-check could compute it in seconds.
holes_found.recommendation: Either (a) update Polaris L to ~2,400 L☉ V-band (or ~2,000-2,500 L☉ depending on source) consistent with the 433 ly distance — this is well within the published range (Polaris bolometric L is often cited 1,260-4,200 L☉ depending on distance choice); or (b) keep 1,260 L☉ and update the distance to ~325 ly; or (c) replace the specific L values with absolute V magnitudes (M_V ≈ −3.6 for Polaris vs M_V ≈ +0.6 for Sigma Octantis) and let the reader convert. Option (c) is most robust because it sidesteps the bolometric-vs-V-band ambiguity that produced the inconsistency.

holes_found.description: Still missing the explicit gradient/preemption argument flagged in c2 (one cycle since c2; not addressed by the new post-hoc paragraph). Our text lists three bright southern stars (Canopus, Achernar, Alpha Centauri) as counterexamples but never frames the dome's implicit prediction as "a smooth declination-dependent extinction gradient that should affect ALL southern stars, with strongest effect nearest the south celestial pole." Without that framing, a dome defender can reframe the claim as pole-only (no gradient required, no quantitative prediction, hence unfalsifiable) — see advocate_mode.best_defense below.
holes_found.recommendation: Add one sentence after the Alpha Centauri example, e.g.: "A maximal-aether-depth-at-rim model implies a smooth declination-dependent extinction gradient: stars at δ = −80° should be more dimmed than stars at δ = −50°, and the south celestial pole region should show a continuous radial fade. No such gradient is observed in any photometric survey at the 1% level — the asymmetry is confined to the random scarcity of luminous stars near the current south celestial pole, exactly what a non-dome distribution predicts."

holes_found.description: Still missing the precession argument (flagged in c2 kernel_of_truth and stronger_arguments, never integrated). Earth's ~25,772-year axial precession is moving the south celestial pole toward Canopus (V=−0.74); around 14,000 AD the south will have a brighter pole star than the north. A geometric aetheric extinction model attached to the dome's rim cannot produce an effect that varies on a precessional timescale — the asymmetry the dome is "predicting" simply tracks current epoch.
holes_found.recommendation: Add to detail_extra (currently null): "Axial precession (~25,772 yr cycle) is moving the south celestial pole toward Canopus (V=−0.74). By ~14,000 AD the south will have a brighter pole star than the north. A geometric aetheric extinction tied to dome rim geometry cannot produce a magnitude asymmetry that flips on a precessional timescale."

holes_found.description: Wording weakness in the new post-hoc paragraph: "we could not locate a pre-registration of the expected value in a timestamping venue independent of the author." This is a weaker phrasing than the case warrants — Sigma Octantis's magnitude has been catalogued for centuries (well before any dome model existed) so the post-hoc test is failed by hundreds of years, not contingent on our search effort. The current wording sounds like "we looked and couldn't find one," which a defender could rebut by producing a 2024 OTS hash (which would still not save the WIN, because the underlying value pre-existed any timestamp).
holes_found.recommendation: Tighten to: "The confirming value (Sigma Octantis V=5.42, Polaris V=1.98) has been in published catalogues since at least the 19th century, and the modern Hipparcos/Gaia distances since the 1990s. No monitor.py entry exists for this WIN; even the dome author's weaker commit-timestamp test cannot apply when the value pre-dates any conceivable commit by decades-to-centuries."

stronger_arguments.description: Wavelength-dependent extinction (color) test: the dome's claimed n(r) → 28.8 at the rim is a refractive index, and any high-n medium with finite scattering should produce wavelength-dependent extinction (Rayleigh λ⁻⁴ for sub-wavelength scatterers, Mie for larger). Southern circumpolar stars should appear systematically REDDER (positive E(B−V) excess) than their spectral classifications predict. Hipparcos B−V and Gaia BP−RP color indices show no such hemispheric color asymmetry. This converts a brightness argument (where dome defenders can hand-wave "random distribution") into a color argument that can't be dismissed by random distribution because color is intrinsic to spectral type.
sources: Gaia DR3 BP−RP photometry (Riello et al. 2021 for the photometric system; Montegriffo et al. 2023 for DR3 BP/RP validation)

stronger_arguments.description: Precession argument (flagged twice now): Earth axial precession with ~25,772 yr cycle moves the south celestial pole toward Canopus (~14,000 AD). The asymmetry is a current-epoch artifact, not a permanent geometric feature of the dome.

OPEN-004 cross-reference: the dome's own published model documents (raw-text/05-model.txt, line 98) flag "OPEN-004: Polaris invisible from southern hemisphere — cutoff formula not derived" as an unsolved open problem. The geometric basis for ANY pole-related stellar effect has not been worked out by the dome author. Citing OPEN-004 explicitly turns the dome's own admission into an argument: the dome model has no derived prediction here, so calling Sigma Octantis's dimness "CONFIRMED" against an underived expectation is by definition retrodictive.

kernel_of_truth.description: The observation is real and historically meaningful (data/wins.json:WIN-033.detail_claim): the south celestial pole genuinely lacks a bright pole star comparable to Polaris, which made southern celestial navigation harder for centuries before the magnetic compass and modern instruments. Asking "why is there no Sigma Octantis at magnitude 1.98?" is a fair question, and a unified explanation should acknowledge it before refuting the dome's answer (data/wins.json:WIN-033.detail_evidence Hipparcos link).
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Stellar physics has the answer: Polaris is an anomalously luminous Cepheid supergiant near the current north celestial pole; no comparably luminous star happens to lie near the current south pole; this is a coincidence of stellar distribution + the current epoch of axial precession (raw-text/05-model.txt line 98 OPEN-004 confirms even the dome has no derivation). The dome model would need a specific extinction mechanism that selectively dims only Sigma Octantis while leaving Canopus (δ=−52.7°, ~13,600 L☉) brilliant at V=−0.74 — see Lens A advocate_mode.best_defense below for the steelmanned reframe and its rebuttal.

deeper_analysis:
Cycle 3 review of WIN-033, triggered by drift detection (detail_verdict_length grew +117% since c2). The growth comes from a newly-added post-hoc-test paragraph applying the dome author's own definition (commit-timestamp before data fetch) and our stricter standard (data not plausibly known to author at write-time). The new paragraph is well-targeted and consistent with our broader Part 3.1b framework — it correctly notes (a) no monitor.py entry exists, so even the dome's weaker timestamp test is inapplicable, and (b) the Hipparcos/Gaia data are public catalogues so the legitimate-novelty carve-out fails. This is a solid expansion of the WIN.

However, the c2 issues are still open: (1) the EDR3-vs-DR3 citation mismatch persists — the new post-hoc paragraph even reuses the wording "Hipparcos main catalogue / Gaia DR3 stellar parameters" while the linked source is Riello et al. 2021 EDR3; (2) the gradient framing is still missing; (3) the precession argument is still in stronger_arguments but never integrated into the visible text. The new content is a net improvement but didn't close the c2 issues.

New this cycle: the quantitative-verification lens caught a numerical inconsistency in the L_Polaris ≈ 1,260 / d=433 ly pairing — those two values together produce Δm = 2.91 magnitudes, off by 0.53 mag from the observed 3.44. The discrepancy is explainable (1,260 L☉ usually goes with d~325 ly in the popular literature) but our text yokes the two figures together. A dome defender computing F ∝ L/d² would catch this. Recommend updating to ~2,400 L☉ at 433 ly, or replacing with absolute V magnitudes.

Advocate-mode produced a non-trivial steelmanned reframe (rated 3): the dome can retreat from "smooth aetheric extinction gradient" to "asymmetry near the pole specifically," which makes the claim unfalsifiable rather than falsified. Our text addresses it implicitly but doesn't preempt the reframe. The gradient sentence proposed in holes_found[2] would close this.

Cross-WIN consistency: WIN-017 (Parallax) cites Gaia DR3 with the correct 2023 release-paper DOI; WIN-033 cites the same dataset with an EDR3 paper URL. This is a mild internal-consistency issue that becomes a citation-credibility issue under LLM scrutiny. WIN-065 (Polaris systematic excess) and WIN-033 both reference Polaris but discuss different phenomena (offset from pole vs. brightness) — no logical conflict, and both verdicts (Refuted by Data) are mutually consistent.

Verdict (Refuted by Data) holds. recommended_action remains minor_edit — five minor-to-moderate issues, no structural rewrite needed. The c2 issues should not have survived to c3; they should have been picked up by the decider's patch queue. Suggest the decider triage these as a minor patch set on the next cycle.

citations_verified: https://www.cosmos.esa.int/web/hipparcos — valid ESA URL for the Hipparcos catalogue
Polaris classification F7Ib supergiant — consistent with SIMBAD/Hipparcos consensus
Sigma Octantis F0III/IV, ~40 L☉, 294 ly — consistent with SIMBAD (V=5.42, parallax 11.08 mas → d=294 ly)
Canopus δ=−52.7°, ~13,600 L☉ — consistent with Hipparcos parallax 10.55 mas → ~310 ly
Achernar δ=−57.2°, B6Vep, ~3,150 L☉, 139 ly — consistent with Hipparcos data
Alpha Centauri δ=−60.8°, G2V+K1V, ~1.5 L☉, 4.37 ly — well-established values
Cross-reference "Part 3.1b" with anchor #posthoc-standard — anchor exists in sections.json:part3.html (verified)
citations_failed: https://www.aanda.org/articles/aa/full_html/2021/05/aa39587-20/aa39587-20.html — URL valid but is Riello et al. 2021 (Gaia EDR3 photometric calibration, A&A 649 A3), not Gaia DR3 as claimed in detail_verdict_text. Same mismatch flagged in c2; not fixed.

Polaris L=1,260 L☉ paired with d=433 ly — internally inconsistent. The 1,260 L☉ figure is bolometric assuming a shorter distance (~325 ly per the older Hipparcos parallax debate). Pairing 1,260 L☉ with the longer 433 ly distance produces a Δm prediction 0.53 mag short of the observed 3.44 mag.
citations_unchecked: Hipparcos main catalogue source for Sigma Octantis V=5.42 — assumed correct but not verified to a specific catalogue line

Gaia DR3 "1.8 billion stars" / "no systematic residuals exceeding 1%" — claim is correct on Gaia documentation but the linked paper (Riello 2021) does not directly contain this 1% statement; the proper citation would be the DR3 photometric validation paper

advocate_mode.best_defense: A fair-minded dome defender accepting our six methodological principles would reframe the WIN as follows: "The dome model never claimed a smooth aetheric extinction gradient that should dim ALL southern stars proportionally to declination. The claim is narrower — that the *south celestial pole specifically* lacks a bright counterpart to Polaris, where the underlying mechanism is some combination of (i) random scarcity of luminous stars in that direction at this epoch and (ii) the dome's rim being symbolically/physically distinguished from the rim's interior. The bright southern stars cited by the critics (Canopus, Achernar, Alpha Centauri at δ ∈ [−60°, −53°]) are within the bulk of the southern stellar distribution, not at the pole. Their existence is fully compatible with a pole-only dimness asymmetry, because the dome's claim isn't a quantitative declination function. The critics' counter is a strawman of a smooth gradient that the dome never asserted." This defense survives a 3 because while it ultimately renders the dome claim unfalsifiable rather than refuted, our text addresses it only by listing three counterexample stars and never explicitly states what a real "aetheric extinction" model should predict (a continuous gradient). A reader following the text in good faith can be persuaded the critics are attacking a model the dome didn't propose.
advocate_mode.preemptive_recommendation: Add a single sentence to detail_verdict_text immediately before the post-hoc paragraph: "A genuine aetheric-depth model entails a smooth declination-dependent extinction gradient — stars at δ=−80° should be progressively dimmer than stars at δ=−50° than stars at δ=0° — and Hipparcos/Gaia photometric residuals across declination show no such gradient at the 1% level. The dome's claim retreats from this prediction to a narrower 'south pole region only' assertion only by becoming unfalsifiable: there is no quantitative declination function and no derived value, so any observed pattern can be retrofitted." This converts an unanswered steelman into a documented preemption.

cross_win_consistency.inconsistencies_found: Citation-only inconsistency, not argumentative: WIN-017 (Parallax = firmament wobble) cites the Gaia DR3 release paper correctly via the Gaia Collaboration 2023 DOI (doi.org/10.1051/0004-6361/202243940), while WIN-033 cites the same 1.8-billion-source Gaia dataset with the Riello et al. 2021 EDR3 paper URL. Both WINs use "Gaia DR3" wording. The arguments are mutually compatible (both rely on real distance-dependent astrometry, both correctly state 1.8 billion sources), but the citation discrepancy means a critic comparing the two WINs side-by-side could flag WIN-033's citation as wrong while WIN-017's is right. WIN-065 (Polaris systematic excess) and WIN-016 (Aberration refractive model) raise no inconsistency — different phenomena, mutually compatible verdicts.

claims_checked: Predicted apparent-magnitude difference Δm from L_Polaris=1,260 L☉ at 433 ly vs L_SOct=40 L☉ at 294 ly using F ∝ L/d²
Implied L_Polaris if observed Δm=3.44 mag is taken as ground truth and SOct values held fixed
Absolute V magnitudes derived from m_V and distance modulus
Implied L_V values from M_V using M_V_sun=4.83
quantitative_verification.computations: F_pol/F_sig = (1260/433²)/(40/294²) = 14.52 → Δm_pred = 2.5·log10(14.52) = 2.91 mag. Observed Δm = 5.42 − 1.98 = 3.44 mag. Discrepancy = 0.53 mag (factor ≈1.62× in flux). Working backwards: L_Polaris implied (holding SOct=40 L☉, distances fixed, observed Δm) = 40 × (433/294)² × 10^((5.42−1.98)/2.5) = 2,062 L☉. Distance moduli: μ_pol = 5·log10(132.6 pc/10 pc) = 5.62, μ_sig = 5·log10(90.3 pc/10 pc) = 4.78. Absolute V: M_V(Polaris) = 1.98 − 5.62 = −3.64, M_V(SOct) = 5.42 − 4.78 = +0.65. L_V from M_V (M_V_sun=4.83): L_V(Polaris) = 10^((4.83−(−3.64))/2.5) = 2,432 L☉; L_V(SOct) = 10^((4.83−0.65)/2.5) = 47 L☉.
quantitative_verification.discrepancies: The cited L_Polaris=1,260 L☉ is inconsistent with the cited d=433 ly. From the apparent V magnitudes and distances, the V-band luminosity must be ≈2,400-2,500 L☉. The 1,260 L☉ figure (popular sources) implicitly uses a shorter Polaris distance ~325 ly. Either revise L upward or revise d downward, or substitute absolute V magnitudes (M_V≈−3.6 vs +0.6) which sidestep the choice. Note the qualitative argument (Polaris is much more luminous) is unaffected — only the precision of "entirely intrinsic luminosity" with these specific numbers is overstated by 0.53 mag.
